Kittur Rani Chenamma

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Kittur Rani Chenamma

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Oct 23, 2024

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

3

Share

In the history of Karnataka, there have been many brave women warriors, who took on the invaders single handedly. Rani Abbaka Chowta of Ullal, who made the Portuguese navy retreat from Mangalore, Keladi Chennamma who beat back Aurangzeb and his Mughal army, Onake Obavva who defended the fort of Chitradurga with just a pestle from the forces of Hyder Ali.

And to such an illustrious pantheon, belonged Rani Chennamma of Kittur. While we know of the Rani of Jhansi’s fight against British, Kittur Rani’s preceded hers much earlier. The backdrop to this was the Doctrine of Lapse, one of the most controversial policies introduced by the British around 1847.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!NU5f!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3d60f1b3-c073-40eb-b19f-478328692516_480x480.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!NU5f!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3d60f1b3-c073-40eb-b19f-478328692516_480x480.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Doctrine of Lapse**

As per the doctrine, if the ruler of any princely state, under the
suzeraintyof the East India Company died without producing a heir, it would have it’s princely status abolished, and be annexed by the British. The Doctrine actually rode over the right of an Indian ruler to choose his succesor, in the event of not having a heir. On top of it the British, decided whether the succesor choosen was competent or not.

To put it very simply, the princely states were essentially slave states to the British, with no rights of their own. The Doctrine began to be implented vigorously by Lord Dalhouise, then Governor General, from 1848 to 1856. Satara was the first such state to be annexed by Dalhousie, in 1848, using this doctrine, though some other princely states too were annexed earlier.

Born on November 14, 1778, in the quiet village of Kakati near Belagavi, Chennamma was destined for greatness. The daughter of Dulappa, the village head, she displayed remarkable courage and skill from a young age, mastering horse riding and archery with ease. At just fifteen, she was married to Raja Mallasarja, the ruler of Kittur, a warrior who himself had fought against Tipu Sultan and famously escaped captivity at Srirangapatna disguised as a wandering sadhu.

Tragedy struck in 1824 when Mallasarja passed away, followed soon after by their only son, Shivalinga Rudra. The double loss left Chennamma devastated, but it also marked the beginning of her transformation into a symbol of resistance.

Grief-stricken after the loss of her husband Raja Mallasarja and her son Shivalinga Rudra, Rani Chennamma threw herself into the administration of Kittur. Supported by wise counselors such as Guru Sidappa and the loyal warrior Sangoli Rayanna, she quickly proved herself to be a capable and respected ruler. Her decision to adopt Shivalingappa as heir was both pragmatic and symbolic,an assertion of Kittur’s sovereignty in the face of colonial interference.

**The Revolt of 1824**

Despite her appeals to St. John Thackeray, Commissioner Chaplin, and Governor Mountstuart Elphinstone, Rani Chennamma’s pleas fell on deaf ears. She sought alliances with neighboring princely states like Kolhapur, but the British were determined to enforce their will. On October 23, 1824, a massive force of 20,797 men and 437 artillery pieces, primarily from the Madras Native Horse Artillery,marched against Kittur.

What followed was a battle that etched her name into history. The warriors of Kittur, led by their indomitable queen, fought with extraordinary valor. Against overwhelming odds, they forced the British to retreat. In the thick of combat, Thackeray himself was shot dead by Amatur Balappa, Chennamma’s loyal bodyguard. Two British officers, Stevenson and William Elliot, were captured and held as hostages. Meanwhile, traitors within, Kannuru Verappa and Saradara Mallappa, who had betrayed the Rani, were slain during the assault.

This stunning victory sent shockwaves across the region. It was not merely a military triumph but a resounding statement that Kittur would not bow easily to colonial dictates. For a brief moment, Rani Chennamma had turned the tide against one of the most powerful empires in the world, proving that courage and conviction could defy even the mightiest armies.

The Final Stand and Legacy

In the aftermath of her stunning victory, Rani Chennamma sought peace. She released the captured British officers, hoping this gesture of goodwill would end hostilities. But the East India Company was relentless. On December 3, 1824, they returned with an even larger army, determined to crush Kittur’s defiance once and for all.

The battle was fierce. Chennamma, aided by her valiant army chief Sangoli Rayanna, led a spirited assault against the invaders. In the chaos, the Sub-Collector of Solapur, Munro, was killed. Yet despite the bravery of Kittur’s defenders, the overwhelming strength of the British forces eventually prevailed. Her loyal lieutenant Gurusidappa was captured, and Rani Chennamma herself, along with her nieces Janaki Bai and Veeramma, was taken prisoner.

Confined within the Bailhongal Fort, the warrior queen spent her final years in captivity. On February 21, 1829, at the age of fifty, she passed away, her body imprisoned, but her spirit forever free.

Rani Chennamma’s resistance was one of the earliest armed uprisings against British rule in India, predating the Revolt of 1857 by decades. Though she did not live to see independence, her courage lit a flame that inspired generations. Today, she is remembered not only as Karnataka’s warrior queen but also as a pioneer of India’s freedom struggle, her legacy honored in statues, songs, and the annual **Kittur Utsav**.

[Kittur Rani Chenamma](https://historyunderyourfeet.wordpress.com/2021/02/20/kittur-rani-chennamma/)
